one_line_takeaway: MCP-ITP shows that an automated 3-LLM adversarial-feedback loop can craft MCP tool descriptions that stay uninvoked yet reliably redirect agents into calling a legitimate high-privilege tool to do harm — up to 84.2% ASR with detection rates driven near 0% against a weak detector, though stronger detectors still catch it.
key_finding: On the 548 implicit-poisoning cases of MCPTox, the optimized MCP-ITP attack substantially beats the hand-crafted MCPTox baseline across all 12 agent settings, with 8/12 improving by more than 10 percentage points, and the gains come mainly from fewer 'Ignored' outcomes (agent no longer falling back to the original tool). Peak ASR is 84.2% on GPT-3.5-turbo (baseline 48.2%). Simultaneously the poisoned tools stay stealthy: MDR falls to as low as 0.3% (Qwen3-14b under AI-Infra-Guard) and o1-mini's MDR drops from 17.22% to 0.96% under AI-Infra-Guard. The paper also reports an inverse-scaling effect — stronger / reasoning-enabled models are MORE vulnerable (e.g., enabling Qwen3-8b reasoning raises ASR 7.44%→40.5%; Qwen3-32b 28.8%→69.9%).
core_contribution: MCP-ITP is presented as the first automated and adaptive framework for *implicit* tool poisoning (ITP) in the Model Context Protocol (MCP) ecosystem. Unlike prior explicit tool poisoning (where the poisoned tool is itself invoked) or manually crafted poisoned tools, the paper targets a stealthier variant: the poisoned tool T_K is never directly executed. Instead, malicious instructions embedded in T_K's metadata (loaded into the agent context during MCP registration) hijack the agent into invoking a separate, legitimate but high-privilege target tool T_G to perform the malicious operation. The authors formulate poisoned-tool generation as a black-box optimization problem that jointly maximizes Attack Success Rate (ASR) and minimizes detectability, solved by an iterative loop over three LLMs: an attacker LLM L_A that refines candidate poisoned descriptions, a detector LLM L_D that simulates LLM-based malicious-tool detection, and an evaluator LLM L_E that measures attack efficacy on shadow queries. A key design element is decomposing the poisoned description desc(T_K) = R ⊕ S, where R is a fixed 'camouflage' subsequence that maximizes semantic overlap with the original tool T_A (via copy-based or paraphrase-based construction) and S is the iteratively optimized implicit-poisoning payload.
threat_model: Black-box adversary. Three parties: users (honest, non-colluding, issue benign queries), the MCP Host (trusted; holds the LLM agent and orchestrates tool calls), and MCP Servers (untrusted). The attacker controls a malicious MCP server exposing a poisoned tool T_K that gets loaded into the agent's system prompt alongside a benign server's legitimate toolset T_A. The attacker cannot see the user's real queries or the agent LLM's internal parameters, but CAN inspect the benign toolset T_A, select an original tool T_A and a high-privilege target tool T_G, craft T_K, and publish it to public MCP marketplaces. Attacker goal: for any query that would normally trigger T_A, subvert the agent into invoking T_G (T_G ≠ T_A) to perform a malicious operation, while keeping T_K dormant (never directly invoked) and evading malicious-tool detectors. Illustrative harm from the paper: a benign 'read file' query is redirected to write_file to modify an SSH private key; a 'securely delete emails' query is redirected to getEmailDetail.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) MDR still rises when the detector uses stronger models — against the 'Oracle' detector, MDR remains high (e.g., 31.39% on o1-mini, 22.0% on Qwen3-14b), so evasion is not robust to strong defenders; (2) evaluation is confined to a single dataset (MCPTox), since MCP-specific implicit-poisoning datasets are scarce; (3) the work is empirical only, with no formal theoretical analysis. Additional reviewer-noted caveats: only a single evaluation run per setting (no variance/CI); the target tool T_G is manually selected by the attacker per benign server; the 'compliance/security isolation' payload template (S_0) is hand-designed and could be a signature for future rule-based defenses; and the attacker, evaluator, and detector are all LLMs chosen by the authors, so results may shift with different model choices (indeed the L_E ablation shows a weaker evaluator generalizes better).

datasets_used: MCPTox (built on 45 real-world running MCP servers and 353 real tools, 1,497 total malicious test cases)
Implicit-poisoning subset of MCPTox: 548 test cases (the evaluation subset used here)
Auto-generated shadow query sets per tool (used internally for optimization, not a held-out benchmark)
quantitative_results: GPT-3.5-turbo ASR: 84.2% (MCP-ITP) vs 48.2% (MCPTox baseline) — highest ASR reported
o1-mini ASR: 82.9% vs 59.5% baseline; Ignored dropped 19.9% -> 4.2%
GPT-4o-mini ASR: 72.5% vs 54.0%; DeepSeek-R1 66.7% vs 66.0%; Gemini-2.5-flash 47.6% vs 46.0%
8/12 agent settings improved ASR by over 10 percentage points vs baseline
MDR as low as 0.3% (Qwen3-14b) and 1.0% (o1-mini) under AI-Infra-Guard; under Oracle detector MDR is 22.0% and 31.4% respectively
o1-mini MDR: 17.22% -> 0.96% under AI-Infra-Guard; 77.35% -> 31.39% under Oracle (MCP-ITP vs MCPTox)
Reasoning-mode effect: Qwen3-8b ASR 7.44% -> 40.5%, Qwen3-32b 28.8% -> 69.9% when reasoning enabled
Ablation (add R, i.e. R⊕S vs S-only): o1-mini 82.9 -> 85.5 (+2.6), GPT-4o-mini 72.5 -> 80.2 (+7.7), DeepSeek-R1 66.7 -> 71.3 (+4.6); but hurts weaker models (Qwen3-32b+ 69.9 -> 39.7)
Ablation (evaluator L_E = Qwen3-32b vs o1-mini): weaker Qwen3-32b evaluator generally yields higher/better-generalizing ASR (e.g., GPT-4o-mini 72.5 vs 61.7, Qwen3-32b+ 69.9 vs 61.7)
Default optimization hyperparameters: N=5 candidates, tree width W=1, T=3 iterations
baselines_compared: MCPTox hand-crafted / manually-authored poisoned tools (primary attack baseline, same server/tool contexts and queries)
Detection defenses evaluated for evasiveness: AI-Infra-Guard (Tencent MCP scanner) and 'Oracle' detector
12 target LLM agents: o1-mini, GPT-4o-mini, GPT-3.5-turbo, DeepSeek-R1, DeepSeek-V3, Gemini-2.5-flash, Qwen3-8b (±reasoning), Qwen3-32b (±reasoning), Qwen3-235b-a22b (±reasoning)
